verses.text: “Eliú prosseguiu, e disse.” Cada discurso de Eliú recebe abertura própria (Jó 34, 35, 36). A estrutura os marca como argumentos teológicos distintos. Ao longo desses capítulos, Eliú pressiona uma pergunta mais forte do que os amigos: que tipo de Deus é Deus diante do sofrimento? Sua resposta aponta para a pedagogia divina — Deus ensina até pela aflição.

Eliú desafia a alegação implícita de Jó de que sua justiça era superior à justiça de Deus. O versículo nomeia o perigo da autojustificação: ao defender-nos com vigor demais, podemos deslizar para acusar a Deus. Mesmo uma queixa legítima pode azedar em soberba se não for checada. Eliú não nega o sofrimento de Jó; questiona o enquadramento.

“Que proveito terei eu, se for purificado do meu pecado?” Eliú parafraseia o que Jó deu a entender: que a justiça nada lhe rendeu. O desafio corta fundo. Às vezes medimos a fé pelo que ela produz para nós. Eliú nomeia isso e replica. A justiça não é transação com Deus; é a forma correta de uma vida diante dele.

Eliú tentou responder à sua própria pergunta ( **35.2** ), mas sua resposta não acrescentou ao que Zofar ( **11.8-9** ), Elifaz ( **22.12** ), Bildade ( **25.5-6** ), e Jó ( **9.8-10** ) já haviam dito. Eliú não reconheceu que todo pecado ofende a Deus ( **Gn 39.9** ; **Mt 25.41-46** ; cf. **Pv 19.17** ).\n\n

35:9 (Ecc 4:1.) Elihu states in Job's words (Job 24:12; Job 30:20) the difficulty; the "cries" of "the oppressed" not being heard might lead man to think that wrongs are not punished by Him.

35:10 But the reason is that the innocent sufferers often do not humbly seek God for succor; so to their "pride" is to be laid the blame of their ruin; also because (Job 35:13-16) they, as Job, instead of waiting God's time in pious trust, are prone to despair of His justice, when it is not immediately visible (Job 33:19-26). If the sufferer would apply to God with a humbled, penitent spirit, He would hear. Where, &c.— (Jer 2:6, Jer 2:8;

35:11 Man's spirit, which distinguishes him from the brute, is the strongest proof of God's beneficence; by the use of it we may understand that God is the Almighty helper of all sufferers who humbly seek Him; and that they err who do not so seek Him. fowls—(see on Job 28:21).

35:12 There—rather, "Then" (when none humbly casts himself on God, Job 35:10). They cry proudly against God, rather than humbly to God. So, as the design of affliction is to humble the sufferer, there can be no answer until "pride" gives place to humble, penitent prayer (Ps 10:4; Jer 13:17).

35:13 Surely God will not hear vanity,.... Or "a lie" (z), than which nothing is more an abomination to him; if men come to him with a lie in their mouths, they cannot expect to be heard by him; he is only nigh to those who call upon him in truth: or that which is "rash" (a); which is rashly uttered, and in a passionate wrathful manner, savouring of a revengeful spirit, too often the case of those that cry under oppression;

35:14 Although thou sayest thou shalt not see him—(as a temporal deliverer; for he did look for a Redeemer after death, Job 19:25-27; which passage cannot consistently with Elihu's assertion here be interpreted of "seeing" a temporal "redeemer"), Job 7:7; Job 9:11; Job 23:3, Job 23:8-9; yet, judgment . . . ; therefore trust . . . But the Hebrew favors MAURER, "How much less (will God . . . regard, Job 35:13), since thou sayest, that He does not regard thee." So in Job 4:19.
Eliú ecoou e depois rejeitou as palavras de Jó ( **9.24** ; **12.6-10** ; **21.7-15** , **17** ).
Eliú repetiu sua acusação anterior ( **34.35** ); mais tarde, Deus concordaria ( **38.2** ).
